data.intent_description: Traffic from 192.168.0.162 to 172.16.0.11 is blackholed by a forwarding loop: R1 and R3 have reciprocal static routes for 172.16.0.0/24 pointing at each other; additionally the intended path via R2 is down (no OSPF neighbor). Desired outcome: break the loop, restore a correct route to 172.16.0.0/24 via R2, and re-establish R1–R2 adjacency so 192.168.0.162 can reach 172.16.0.11.

plan.problem: Reciprocal static routes on R1 and R3 create a forwarding loop/blackhole for 172.16.0.0/24; R2 path is currently unavailable (no OSPF neighbor on R1–R2).
plan.fix_summary: 1) Remove the two incorrect static routes that point R1 and R3 at each other. 2) Add a corrective static route on R1 pointing 172.16.0.0/24 toward R2 (assumed next-hop 10.0.0.2). 3) Ensure R1 Serial0/1/0 is administratively up to restore adjacency to R2.

messages.content:
What was observed
- R1 and R2 had no OSPF adjacency on the 10.0.0.0/30 link and traffic from 192.168.0.162 to 172.16.0.11 was being blackholed.

Diagnosis (root cause)
- OSPF area mismatch: the 10.0.0.0/30 link was in area 0 on R1 but area 1 on R2, preventing the R1–R2 adjacency and R1 from learning R2's 172.16.0.0/24.

Changes made
- Removed reciprocal static routes that could create a forwarding loop (on R1 and R3).
- Added static route on R1: 172.16.0.0/24 via 10.0.0.2.
- On R2, added OSPF network statement for 10.0.0.0/30 into area 0 and ensured the Serial0/1/0 interface is up.

Verification
- Passed. R1 now sees neighbor 2.2.2.2 (10.0.0.2) in state FULL and R2 sees 1.1.1.1 (10.0.0.1) FULL. Interfaces on both ends are admin/up and oper/up. R2 is advertising 172.16.0.0/24 into OSPF; R1 has a route to 172.16.0.0/24 via 10.0.0.2.

Remaining
- None identified. The R1–R2 adjacency and route to 172.16.0.0/24 via R2 are restored.
